Airports

Hundreds of motors. One missed bag starts a cascade.

Baggage handling systems run 16 to 20 hours a day with zero tolerance for failure. Most conveyor motors have no condition monitoring at all. ESA detects bearing wear, gearbox faults, and belt degradation from the motor control cabinet, weeks before breakdown.

9 / 9
Faults detected at Schiphol Airport
>5 hrs
Baggage belt downtime saved
>90%
Faults prevented at Stansted

Schiphol: 12-month trial on baggage handling conveyors (Samotics published case study). Stansted: deployment across arrival and departure hall BHS systems (Samotics/Aviation Week, 2025).

Start your asset audit See airport results
Trusted by leading airports
The problem

Downtime is measured in missed flights and stranded bags

Airport equipment runs near-continuously in environments that make condition monitoring harder than any factory.

Baggage handling runs until it breaks
A large airport operates 500 to 1,000+ conveyor motors across check-in, sorting, screening, and reclaim. Bearing wear causes 51 to 67% of motor failures. SCADA detects a stopped conveyor. It does not detect the bearing that will fail in three weeks. One motor failure cascades upstream and downstream, choking hundreds of connected units.
Maintenance windows measured in hours
All preventive work must fit between the last departure and first morning check-in. That is 4 to 6 hours. Sensor installation on running conveyors is not feasible. When maintenance is deferred because windows are too short, equipment degrades past the point of quick repair. Emergency maintenance costs 3 to 5 times more than planned.
Motors spread across terminals
BHS drives, HVAC fans, escalators, and walkway motors are distributed across multiple buildings and levels. Route-based vibration collection requires technicians to visit each asset monthly. Travel between assets dominates analyst time. For widely spaced equipment, this is slow, expensive, and incomplete.
Noise floor defeats vibration baselines
Airports generate vibration from aircraft, passenger traffic, HVAC systems, and structural resonance in multi-storey buildings. This creates baseline variability that makes vibration monitoring harder than in a purpose-built industrial plant. Distinguishing a developing fault from environmental noise requires careful baseline establishment that most programmes lack.
Why SAM4

Why SAM4 works for airports

Electrical Signature Analysis monitors every motor-driven asset from the motor control cabinet. No sensors on the conveyors, escalators, or fans.

No sensors on the asset
SAM4 clips onto power cables inside the MCC in the electrical room. No work on conveyor infrastructure, no access to passenger areas, no disruption to baggage flow. Install while the BHS is running.
Every motor from one location
Hundreds of conveyor drives, AHU fans, escalator motors, and walkway drives monitored from per-zone electrical rooms. No technician routes across terminals. No per-asset sensor economics limiting fleet coverage.
Detects what SCADA and vibration miss
SCADA detects operational faults. Vibration struggles with airport noise floors and small conveyor motors. ESA detects bearing wear, gearbox degradation, belt slippage, and rotor bar defects through motor current modulation. Weeks of advance warning, not hours.
Works on small BHS motors
Baggage handling motors range from 0.55 to 5.6 kW. Vibration monitoring economics break down at this scale. ESA is highly effective on small motors because high current density produces clear fault signatures. No per-point cost barrier.

Vibration route collection visits each motor monthly. ESA monitors every motor continuously. For airports running 16 to 20 hours a day, that difference is the gap between detecting a fault and missing a flight.

What we monitor

Critical assets in airport operations

SAM4 monitors any AC motor-driven equipment from the MCC. These are the asset types that matter most at airports.

Baggage handling conveyors
Check-in, sorting, screening, and reclaim conveyors. SAM4 detects bearing wear, gearbox faults, belt slippage, rotor bar defects, and motor overload across tilt-tray, cross-belt, and DCV systems.
Conveyor monitoring details →
HVAC and ventilation fans
Air handling units, exhaust fans, pressurisation fans, and cooling tower motors. HVAC accounts for 40 to 60% of terminal energy. SAM4 detects bearing wear, belt degradation, and stator winding faults across 24/7 systems.
Fan monitoring details →
Escalators and moving walkways
Drive motor, worm gear reducer, and chain drive assemblies. SAM4 detects motor bearing wear, gearbox worm wear, brake drag, and chain elongation. Step chain and roller bearing wear account for 35% of escalator shutdowns.
Transmission monitoring details →
Proof

Airport case studies

Featured case study

Schiphol Airport: 9 of 9 faults detected in 12-month trial

During a 12-month trial on baggage handling conveyors, SAM4 detected all 9 developing faults before any caused unplanned downtime. More than 5 hours of baggage belt downtime saved. Schiphol expanded deployment across the full production system based on trial results.

9 / 9
Faults detected in advance
>5 hrs
Belt downtime saved
Full deploy
Expanded after trial

12-month trial on baggage handling conveyor drives at Amsterdam Airport Schiphol. Fault detection confirmed against maintenance records. Published Samotics case study.

Read the full Schiphol case study →
>90%
London Stansted Airport
SAM4 deployed across dozens of baggage handling systems in arrival and departure halls. 80% of downtime was mechanical. SAM4 identifies and prevents over 90% of potential faults up to 5 months in advance.
Read announcement →
30–50%
Industry benchmark
Reduction in unplanned downtime documented across airport predictive maintenance programmes. Emergency maintenance costs 3 to 5 times more than planned interventions.
See all results →
10:1–30:1
Airport PdM ROI
Return on investment within 12 to 18 months for airport condition monitoring programmes. At $10,000 to $100,000 per hour of BHS downtime, a single prevented failure covers the annual monitoring cost.
See all results →

Stansted: Samotics announcement and Aviation Week (2025). Industry benchmarks: OxMaint airport PdM analysis, FAA delay cost estimates ($100/minute), EASA ground delay estimates. ROI ranges from vendor and trade sources.

Ready to see what SAM4 finds at your airport? Request a demo →
Installation

No sensors on the belts. No work in passenger areas.

SAM4 installs in the electrical room. MCC cabinets sit in staff-only basement plant rooms or per-zone electrical rooms. No disruption to passengers or baggage operations.

1
Clip on at the MCC Clip-on current transformers attach to the motor power cable inside the cabinet. Install while the BHS is running. Minutes per motor. No conveyor shutdown.
2
Connect to the network Secure cellular gateway transmits data to the cloud. No airport IT network integration required for initial deployment. Wi-Fi and Ethernet also supported.
3
Monitor from anywhere The SAM4 dashboard shows asset health, fault progression, and energy performance. Pilot 20 to 30 priority motors in the first week. Full fleet in 4 to 6 weeks.

The results speak for themselves: it just works.

Marcel den Blanken, Baggage Service Manager, Schiphol Airport
Common questions

Airport monitoring FAQ

SCADA detects operational faults: conveyor stopped, motor overheating. ESA detects degradation: bearing wear developing over weeks, gearbox tooth damage progressing toward failure. 51 to 67% of motor failures are bearing-related. SCADA misses nearly all of them in early stages.

Predictive monitoring shifts the paradigm. Instead of firefighting at midnight, you know 3 to 4 weeks in advance which motor needs attention. You schedule the repair into the next available window rather than reacting to a failure. Emergency maintenance costs 3 to 5 times more than planned work.

Yes. ESA is highly effective on small motors. High current density produces clear fault signatures. Unlike vibration sensors, ESA requires no physical access to the motor and no per-point sensor economics. This makes it ideal for BHS fleets where hundreds of small motors need coverage.

SAM4 runs independently with optional API integration into BMS or CMMS. Fault alerts can trigger automatic work orders in your maintenance system. No dependency on existing SCADA infrastructure. Initial deployment requires no airport IT network integration.

OEM contracts provide scheduled preventive maintenance and emergency repair. They do not provide continuous early detection between visits. ESA adds the monitoring layer that reduces emergency callouts and prevents the 30 to 50% of unplanned downtime that develops between scheduled service intervals.

SAM4 reads electrical signatures from the MCC. It is read-only with no commands to equipment. The system runs on an isolated network segment, separate from baggage SCADA. Samotics holds ISO 27001 and ISO 9001 certification. Data transmission uses secure cellular connectivity by default.

Get started

Monitor every conveyor, fan, and escalator. No sensors on the belts.

Start with your highest-traffic baggage handling line. See results within weeks. Scale across every terminal.

Start your asset audit Talk to an airport engineer

Monitoring the assets your sensors can't reach.
